requirements.requirement: A private run identity and prepared record exist before provider launch.
One run ID joins the manifest, artifact, and Longhouse origin environment.
Lifecycle, result, capture, provider-state, and archive axes use independent validated vocabularies.
Terminal outcome is committed once after result projection and later axes cannot invent a different provider outcome.
Readers retain additive raw fields, normalize unknown enums, and reject unknown schema majors.
Go-preview provider-version records remain readable under the canonical provider_tool_version field.
Every new artifact explicitly identifies the Go persistence-contract revision used by field and migration gates.
Run directories and evidence files are private independent of caller umask.
Unavailable and symlink artifact roots fail closed before provider execution.
Raw stdout and stderr are drained to private evidence while interpretation remains available.
Every atomic-file persistence boundary propagates failure.
result.json is durable before the terminal manifest commit point.
A sorted per-file hash manifest is persisted and its digest binds the exact closed evidence set; retirement rejects missing, extra, duplicate, copied, or traversal-bearing evidence.
Persistence failure after launch does not kill the provider, discard a returned answer, or erase process ownership needed for timeout cleanup.
A killed Hatch leaves an inspectable nonterminal artifact and raw evidence without outcome guessing.
Large provider output is bounded in public memory while complete raw evidence remains on disk.
Prompts and credential values are structurally excluded from persisted argv and mismatched metadata fails closed.
Traversal and symlink substitution are rejected by writer, evidence, and inspection paths.
Claude structured success, identity, fallback text, and structured errors are interpreted without losing raw events.
Cursor identity, terminal success/error, and duplicate tool progress are handled without recovery claims.
OpenCode identity, transient recovery, state snapshot, concurrency, and structured errors are evidence-backed.
Only allowlisted OpenCode state is copied into a frozen snapshot; auth, cache, unrelated files, and symlinks are excluded, and late writes to retired descriptors cannot change the snapshot.
Inspection and recovery hints require a recorded provider tool version and remain structured, risk-labelled, and same-version-bound.
Recognized structured data with no terminal interpretation emits the locked adapter drift warning.
Doctor resolves the normal credential authority and detects unavailable Cursor, every Codex alias, and every OpenRouter stable alias before a provider run.
Expert uses HTTP evidence, response identity, polling snapshots, timeout without remote cancellation, and pre/post-identity errors.
Missing provider binaries produce durable launch_failed artifacts.
Nonzero exits preserve stderr and exact exit status in a failed terminal record.
Structured errors and missing terminal markers fail while preserving output and warning provenance.
Timeout preserves partial evidence, kills the owned process group, bounds pipe cleanup, and does not claim descendant certainty.
Parent cancellation or an entrypoint signal fails closed before launch or kills the owned process group, commits cancelled, and exits 130; a concurrently completed answer wins the race.
Local list and inspect require no provider credentials and enumerate exact current artifact files.
Inspection reports current process/orphan evidence without mutating a nonterminal lifecycle.
Current, legacy OpenCode, and legacy Expert artifacts are readable without mutation.
Surfaced and raw subprocess commands produce the common canonical nested run projection.
Expert produces the same canonical run axes without fake process evidence.
Every frozen Python behavior has an executable proof or explicit retired/obsolete disposition.
Supported release targets build reproducibly and install with explicit rollback.
